nodes.title: Jamestown Settlement
nodes.description: The first permanent English settlement is established in Virginia, initiating colonial growth.

nodes.title: House of Burgesses Established
nodes.description: The first representative legislative assembly in the American colonies is established in Virginia, laying seeds for representative democracy.

nodes.title: Mayflower Compact Signed
nodes.description: Pilgrim settlers sign the compact aboard the Mayflower, creating one of the earliest frameworks for self-governance in the New World.

nodes.title: Harvard College Founded
nodes.description: The first institution of higher learning in the American colonies is established in Massachusetts.

nodes.title: Salem Witch Trials
nodes.description: A series of hearings and prosecutions of people accused of witchcraft in colonial Massachusetts, reflecting intense social and religious anxieties.

nodes.title: First Great Awakening Begins
nodes.description: A series of Christian revivals sweeps the colonies, fostering a shared American identity and challenging traditional authority.

nodes.title: French and Indian War Begins
nodes.description: Conflict begins between Great Britain and France (with Native American allies) for control of North America, leading to massive British debt and colonial taxes.

nodes.title: Boston Tea Party
nodes.description: American colonists protest British taxation by dumping tea into Boston Harbor, fueling revolutionary sentiment.

nodes.title: Battles of Lexington and Concord
nodes.description: The first military engagements of the American Revolutionary War occur in Massachusetts, starting the armed conflict.

nodes.title: Declaration of Independence
nodes.description: The Continental Congress declares independence from Great Britain, starting the Revolutionary War.

nodes.title: Treaty of Paris Signed
nodes.description: Great Britain formally recognizes American independence, officially ending the Revolutionary War.

nodes.title: US Constitution Drafted
nodes.description: Delegates gather at Philadelphia to draft the Constitution, establishing the federal republic.

nodes.title: Bill of Rights Ratified
nodes.description: The first ten amendments to the Constitution are ratified, securing key personal freedoms and limiting government power.

nodes.title: Louisiana Purchase
nodes.description: The United States purchases Louisiana Territory from France, doubling the national domain.

nodes.title: Lewis and Clark Expedition
nodes.description: Meriwether Lewis and William Clark embark on an expedition to map and explore the newly acquired western territories.

nodes.title: War of 1812
nodes.description: The United States declares war against Great Britain over maritime rights and trade restrictions, consolidating its national sovereignty.

nodes.title: Missouri Compromise
nodes.description: Federal legislation admits Maine as a free state and Missouri as a slave state, attempting to balance political power between North and South.

nodes.title: Monroe Doctrine Declared
nodes.description: President James Monroe warns European powers against further colonization or intervention in the Americas.

nodes.title: Erie Canal Completed
nodes.description: The canal connects the Great Lakes with the Atlantic Ocean via the Hudson River, sparking economic expansion and trade.

nodes.title: Battle of the Alamo
nodes.description: Texan defenders are defeated by the Mexican army at the Alamo, becoming a symbol of heroic resistance in the Texas Revolution.

nodes.title: Trail of Tears
nodes.description: The forced relocation of Cherokee and other Native American nations results in thousands of deaths along the journey west.

nodes.title: Manifest Destiny & Texas
nodes.description: Texas is annexed as the U.S. embraces the ideology of westward continental expansion.

nodes.title: California Gold Rush
nodes.description: Gold discovered at Sutter's Mill triggers a massive migration of over 300,000 people to California, accelerating western integration.

nodes.title: Seneca Falls Convention
nodes.description: The first women's rights convention launches the organized struggle for women's suffrage and equality.

nodes.title: Compromise of 1850
nodes.description: A package of five bills defuses a major political confrontation over territories acquired during the Mexican-American War, including a stricter Fugitive Slave Act.

nodes.title: Abraham Lincoln Elected President
nodes.description: Lincoln's victory triggers the secession of several Southern states, directly leading to the outbreak of the Civil War.

nodes.title: American Civil War
nodes.description: Conflict erupts between the Union and the seceded southern Confederacy over slavery and federal sovereignty.

nodes.title: Homestead Act Signed
nodes.description: President Lincoln signs the act, granting 160 acres of public land to settlers who improve it, accelerating westward settlement.

nodes.title: Emancipation Proclamation
nodes.description: President Abraham Lincoln declares all slaves in confederate-held territory free, redefining the war's purpose.

nodes.title: Gettysburg Address
nodes.description: President Lincoln delivers a historic speech at Gettysburg, redefining the Civil War as a struggle for human equality and democracy.

nodes.title: Reconstruction Era Begins
nodes.description: The nation begins the complex process of rebuilding the South and integrating millions of newly freed African Americans into society.

nodes.title: Alaska Purchased from Russia
nodes.description: The United States acquires Alaska from the Russian Empire for $7.2 million, adding a vast, resource-rich territory.

nodes.title: Transcontinental Railroad Completed
nodes.description: The Golden Spike is driven in Promontory Summit, Utah, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific coasts by rail.

nodes.title: Gilded Age Begins
nodes.description: An era of rapid economic and industrial expansion, coupled with immigration and urbanization.

nodes.title: Sherman Antitrust Act
nodes.description: The first federal action to limit monopolies and cartels, laying the foundation for modern corporate regulation.

nodes.title: Wounded Knee Massacre
nodes.description: U.S. cavalry troops massacre nearly 300 Lakota Sioux, marking the tragic end of the armed Indian Wars on the Great Plains.

nodes.title: Spanish-American War
nodes.description: A brief conflict ending Spanish colonial rule in the Americas and establishing the US as a global power with overseas territories.

nodes.title: Wright Brothers' First Flight
nodes.description: Orville and Wilbur Wright achieve the first successful sustained, controlled, powered flight of an airplane at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.

nodes.title: Pure Food and Drug Act Signed
nodes.description: Providing for federal inspection of meat products and forbidding the manufacture or sale of adulterated food or medicine.

nodes.title: Panama Canal Completed
nodes.description: The canal officially opens, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, drastically reducing shipping times and projecting global power.

nodes.title: U.S. Enters World War I
nodes.description: Abandoning neutrality, the United States joins the Allied Powers, swinging the conflict in their favor.

nodes.title: Women's Suffrage Ratified
nodes.description: The 19th Amendment is ratified, securing women the constitutionally guaranteed right to vote.

nodes.title: Roaring Twenties Begins
nodes.description: A period of economic prosperity, rapid cultural changes, and social dynamism, characterized by jazz music and flapper culture.

nodes.title: Great Depression
nodes.description: The Wall Street stock crash launches a decade of deep economic contraction and widespread poverty.

nodes.title: FDR's New Deal Programs
nodes.description: President Franklin D. Roosevelt launches massive government relief, recovery, and financial reform initiatives.

nodes.title: Lend-Lease Act
nodes.description: The US begins providing critical military aid to foreign Allies, shifting away from strict isolationism prior to entering WWII.

nodes.title: US Enters World War II
nodes.description: Following the attack on Pearl Harbor, the United States declares war and joins the Allies.

nodes.title: D-Day & Normandy Landings
nodes.description: Allied forces launch the largest amphibious invasion in history on the beaches of Normandy, establishing a crucial Western Front against Nazi Germany.

nodes.title: Atomic Bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
nodes.description: The United States drops atomic bombs on two Japanese cities, leading to the unconditional surrender of Japan and ending World War II.

nodes.title: Truman Doctrine Declared
nodes.description: President Truman pledges support to democratic nations against authoritarian forces, formalizing the Cold War geopolitical structure.

nodes.title: Marshall Plan Enacted
nodes.description: The US launches a massive economic aid initiative to rebuild devastated Western European economies and halt communist expansion.

nodes.title: Korean War Outbreak
nodes.description: The US leads a UN coalition supporting South Korea, marking the first major hot conflict of the Cold War.

nodes.title: Cuban Missile Crisis
nodes.description: A direct and dangerous confrontation between the United States and the Soviet Union, bringing the two superpowers closest to nuclear conflict.

nodes.title: Assassination of JFK
nodes.description: President John F. Kennedy is assassinated in Dallas, Texas, shocking the nation and elevating Lyndon B. Johnson to the presidency.

nodes.title: Civil Rights Movement
nodes.description: The landmark Brown v. Board decision initiates a decades-long struggle for racial equality led by activists like Martin Luther King Jr.

nodes.title: March on Washington
nodes.description: Civil rights leaders organize a massive demonstration where Martin Luther King Jr. delivers his famous 'I Have a Dream' speech.

nodes.title: Voting Rights Act Signed
nodes.description: Landmark federal legislation that prohibits racial discrimination in voting, securing franchise for millions of minority voters.

nodes.title: Vietnam War Troop Deployment
nodes.description: The United States deploys combat troops to South Vietnam, escalating its military role in the containment of Communism.

nodes.title: Apollo Moon Landing
nodes.description: Apollo 11 astronaut Neil Armstrong becomes the first human to step on the lunar surface.

nodes.title: Watergate Scandal
nodes.description: A major political scandal following a break-in at the DNC headquarters, leading to the resignation of President Richard Nixon.

nodes.title: Roe v. Wade Supreme Court Decision
nodes.description: The Supreme Court rules that the Constitution protects a pregnant woman's liberty to choose to have an abortion, establishing abortion rights nationwide.

nodes.title: Camp David Accords Signed
nodes.description: Historic agreements signed by Egypt and Israel, brokered by President Jimmy Carter, leading to a peace treaty between the two nations.

nodes.title: Ronald Reagan Elected President
nodes.description: Reagan's victory ushers in a conservative political era marked by tax cuts, deregulation, and a strong anti-Soviet foreign policy.

nodes.title: Challenger Space Shuttle Disaster
nodes.description: The Space Shuttle Challenger breaks apart 73 seconds into its flight, killing all seven crew members and temporarily halting the shuttle program.

nodes.title: Fall of the Berlin Wall
nodes.description: The opening of the Berlin Wall symbolizes the collapse of communist regimes in Eastern Europe and the impending end of the Cold War.

nodes.title: September 11 Attacks
nodes.description: Al-Qaeda terrorists hijack passenger planes and strike the World Trade Center and Pentagon, launching the War on Terror.

nodes.title: Digital & Mobile Boom
nodes.description: The release of the first iPhone accelerates the transition into the modern digital age, mobile internet, and global app networks.

nodes.title: Barack Obama Elected
nodes.description: Barack Obama is elected as the 44th President of the United States, becoming the nation's first African American president.

nodes.title: Affordable Care Act Signed
nodes.description: President Obama signs the major healthcare reform bill into law, expanding health insurance coverage to millions of Americans.

nodes.title: Same-Sex Marriage Legalized Nationwide
nodes.description: In Obergefell v. Hodges, the Supreme Court rules that same-sex couples have a constitutional right to marry.

nodes.title: COVID-19 Pandemic Outbreak
nodes.description: The novel coronavirus triggers a global health crisis, leading to widespread lockdowns, school closures, and economic disruption.
